coredata.dc:title: Enhancing electrochromic performance and stability: A novel ionic complex based on carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Kim, Jaeheon
Choi, Woojun
Kim, Tae Hyun
Lim, Hong Chul
coredata.dc:description: Electrolytes are crucial in electrochemical applications such as rechargeable batteries, supercapacitors, fuel cells, and electrochromic devices (ECDs). Therefore, developing new electrolytes with high ionic conductivity, electrochemical stability, and environmental friendliness is essential for advancing next-generation electrochemical systems. Herein, we developed a novel type of ionic complexes composed of multivalent carbon dot polyanions (C dot) and equivalent alkali cations such as Li+ (Li-C dot), Na+ (Na-C dot), and K+ (K-C dot). As an electrolyte, the K-C dot demonstrated superior electrochromic behavior of Prussian blue (PB)–a promising electrochromic material for ECDs–compared to commercially available KCl. The PB thin films exhibit high coloration efficiency (η) of 103.5 cm2 C−1, good optical density (OD) of 0.90, and fast color switching time (1.5 and 2.6 s for the bleaching and coloring processes, respectively). Furthermore, the PB thin films exhibit long-term stability, retaining 97 % of the initial optical contrast (ΔT) even after 1,000 color switching cycles. The electrochromic performance of PB is influenced by counter cations such as Li+, Na+, and K+, which occupy the interstitial cavities in the lattice of PB during the electrochromic process. These results suggest that to improve the electrochromic performance of PB thin films in ECDs, it is essential to balance the migration of counter cations into and out of the lattice channels of the PB structure with the electron transfer between the PB thin film and the electrode substrate to maintain the charge neutrality of PB. We anticipate that our study will enhance the understanding of the electrochromic process in ECDs and provide new insights into the design of electrolytes for electrochemical systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ionic complex
Electrolyte
Prussian blue
Electrochromic

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Prussian blue thin film on indium tin oxide substrate

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF), with the grant funded by Korea Government (MSIT and MOE) (No. NRF-2022R1F1A1064007). This research was also supported by \u201CRegional Innovation Strategy (RIS)\u201D through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (MOE) in 2024(2022RIS-005). This work was also supported by the Soonchunhyang University Research Fund.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images (inserted image: high resolution TEM image of K-C dot and corresponding 2D FFT image), (b) FT-IR spectrum, and (c) XPS spectrum of the narrow scan of K 2p and C1s of the prepared K-C dot.
(a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum (Inserted image is a photograph of the electrodeposited PB/ITO) and (b) absorbance and optical transmittance of PB thin films as a function of electrodeposition time.
SEM images of PB thin films electrodeposited obtained from (a) plan-view and (b) cross-sectional view.

(a) The electrochemical behaviors of PB/ITO in an aqueous solution containing 0.5 M K-C dot at a scan rate of 20 mV/s and (b) Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) of PB/ITO under ac amplitude of 5 mV as a function of electrolytes (Ag/AgCl: reference electrode, Pt wire: counter electrode, and PB thin film: working electrode).
Under 0.5 M electrolyte conditions, (a) in situ optical transmittance and (b) optical density as a function of operating cycles for PB/ITO at 700 nm.
Electrochromic properties of PB thin films as a function of electrolytes.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: All electrochemical data were measured at RT. A three-electrode system is used (PB thin film: working electrode, Pt wire: counter electrode, and Ag/AgCl: reference electrode). Transmittance values were measured at 700 nm. The transmittance value is defined as the intensity of transmittance to reach 90 and 10 % of the final color changing state in transmittance between the steady PB and PW states, respectively. The color switching time (t c and t b) refer to transmittance reaching 10 % and 90 % of its full modulation at 700 nm absorbance of PB thin films.

head.ce:title: Enhancing electrochromic performance and stability: A novel ionic complex based on carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Medical Chemistry, Yonsei University, Wonju 26493, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Medical Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Medical Chemistry, Yonsei University, Wonju 26493, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Soonchunhyang University, Asan 31538, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Soonchunhyang University, Asan 31538, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutics and Biopharmacy, Sangji University, Wonju 26339, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutics and Biopharmacy, Sangji University, Wonju 26339, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The ionic complex of K-C dot used as an electrolyte demonstrates high electrochromic performance and stability for Prussian blue.

Electrolytes are crucial in electrochemical applications such as rechargeable batteries, supercapacitors, fuel cells, and electrochromic devices (ECDs). Therefore, developing new electrolytes with high ionic conductivity, electrochemical stability, and environmental friendliness is essential for advancing next-generation electrochemical systems. Herein, we developed a novel type of ionic complexes composed of multivalent carbon dot polyanions (C dot) and equivalent alkali cations such as Li+ (Li-C dot), Na+ (Na-C dot), and K+ (K-C dot). As an electrolyte, the K-C dot demonstrated superior electrochromic behavior of Prussian blue (PB)–a promising electrochromic material for ECDs–compared to commercially available KCl. The PB thin films exhibit high coloration efficiency (η) of 103.5 cm2 C−1, good optical density (OD) of 0.90, and fast color switching time (1.5 and 2.6 s for the bleaching and coloring processes, respectively). Furthermore, the PB thin films exhibit long-term stability, retaining 97 % of the initial optical contrast (ΔT) even after 1,000 color switching cycles. The electrochromic performance of PB is influenced by counter cations such as Li+, Na+, and K+, which occupy the interstitial cavities in the lattice of PB during the electrochromic process. These results suggest that to improve the electrochromic performance of PB thin films in ECDs, it is essential to balance the migration of counter cations into and out of the lattice channels of the PB structure with the electron transfer between the PB thin film and the electrode substrate to maintain the charge neutrality of PB. We anticipate that our study will enhance the understanding of the electrochromic process in ECDs and provide new insights into the design of electrolytes for electrochemical systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochromic devices (ECDs), which modulate optical properties through the redox processes of an electrochromic material, are considered highly attractive for various applications, including electrochemical sensors, smart windows, and automobile mirrors [1–4]. Prussian Blue (PB), a promising electrochromic material for ECDs, is composed of a face-centered cubic (FCC) structure in which Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions are alternately coordinated to carbon and nitrogen atoms through cyanide groups within the unit cell [5]. The electrochromic reactions of PB is controlled by the applied electrical voltage or current, resulting in any charge flowing through the external circuit [2]. However, PB is known to be sensitive to the type of electrolyte. PB is susceptible to attack by Cl− and CO3 2− anions in electrolytes, leading to rapid degradation. Furthermore, the lattice structure of PB features channels of limited size (< 5.1 Å), which may constrain the accommodation of compensating counterions required for maintaining charge balance during redox processes [6,7]. Therefore, one of the key factors in enhancing the electrochromic performance of PB–including high coloration efficiency, rapid color switching, and good electrochemical stability–is the selection of appropriate electrolytes. The electrolyte must exhibit several critical properties: 1) high ionic conductivity to minimize polarization within the ECDs and optimize performance; 2) efficient ion transport to facilitate the intercalation and deintercalation of charge-compensating counterions; and 3) electrochemical inertness to ensure the stability of the ECDs when dissolved. Over the past few decades, research on electrolytes has primarily focused on developing ionic complexes to provide acceptable solubility and electrochemical stability [5,8,9]. Recently, weakly coordinating anions (WCAs) containing nitrogen, phosphorus, and boron have garnered attention as potential electrolytes due to their relatively large molecular size and charge delocalization [10,11]. These anions are expected to offer lower dissociation energy compared to conventional anions such as PF6 −, BF4 −, and ClO4 −, potentially resulting in higher ionic conductivity. However, these electrolytes based on WCAs often induce electrolysis and side reactions, resulting in deteriorated stability and reliability of the ECDs [7,12].

In this study, we developed a novel type of ionic complexes composed of multivalent carbon dot polyanions (C dot) and equivalent alkali cations such as Li+ (Li-C dot), Na+ (Na-C dot), and K+ (K-C dot). We have demonstrated that using K-C dot as an electrolyte significantly enhances the electrochemical behaviors of PB thin films electrodeposited on indium tin oxide (ITO) electrode. The PB thin films exhibit superior electrochromic performance with high coloration efficiency (η, 103.5 cm2 C−1), good optical density (OD, 0.90), and long-term switching stability (less than ∼ 3 % decrease in ΔT after 1000 cycles), compared to commercially available KCl used as a control.

All the chemical reagents used in this study were commercially available and used without further purification. Carbon nanofiber, sulfuric acid (H2SO4), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3), lithium carbonate (Li2CO3), potassium carbonate (K2CO3), iron(III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O) and potassium ferricyanide (K3[Fe(CN)6]) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Nitric acid (HNO3), ethanol, and potassium chloride (KCl) were purchased from Daejung Chemical & Metals Co., Ltd. (Siheung, Korea).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Prussian blue thin film on indium tin oxide substrate
ce:section.ce:para: Prussian blue (PB) thin films on ITO/glass substrate (PB/ITO) were deposited by the chronoamperometry (CA) method in an electrochemical cell, which consisted of a three-electrode system (a reference electrode (Ag/AgCl), counter electrode (Pt wire), and working electrode (ITO/glass substrate; SnI; 8–12 Ω)) in aqueous solution containing 0.05 M HCl, 0.05 M K3Fe(CN)6, and 0.05 M FeCl3·6H2O. The PB thin films were subjected to 3 cycles at 0.72–0.77 V at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 from 60 to 300 s. The resultant PB thin films were rinsed with DI water and kept away from dust and grease until the experiment was conducted. The average thickness of PB thin films was 320 nm.

The electrochemical experiments were performed with a CH instrument (760E electrochemical analyzer) using a three-electrode system, at room temperature (RT). All potentials were reported versus Ag/AgCl reference electrode. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed with 5 mV amplitude in a frequency range from 1 to 100 kHz. The optical properties of prepared ionic complexes and spectroscopy testing of electrodeposited PB/ITO were conducted by absorption spectra (Beckman, DU650). In situ spectroelectrochemical properties of PB/ITO were carried out on wavedriver 200 with spectroelectrochemistry bundle (Pine research, WD200-SEPC). Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was conducted using a Nicolet 6700 (Thermo scientific, USA). Functional groups and chemical composition of prepared ionic complexes based on C dot were analysed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Kratos, AXIS Supra) using a monochromatic Alkα (15 kV, 375 W). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) (JEOL, KEO-2100) was performed at 200 kV for the acquisition of C dot images. Scanning electron microscope was used (SEM) (Hitachi, S-4300) to confirm the morphology and thickness of PB/ITO. The electrochromic properties including coloration/bleaching time, optical density, and coloration efficiency were measured by transmittance variation at a wavelength of 700 nm with alternately applied potential of −0.16 and 0.4 V, 50 % duty cycle for 10 s in each state.

The ionic complexes based on C dot used in this work were synthesized through the hydrothermal oxidation process of carbon nanofibers, following the procedure described in our previous report [10]. HRTEM was conducted to determine the size and shape of the prepared K-C dot. The K-C dot, exhibiting a diffraction pattern consistent with sp2-conjugated carbon structure at the core, was observed with a diameter of approximately 8.7 nm, as shown in Fig. 1 a. The amorphous structure observed at the edges is due to the presence of oxygen functional groups such as CO and O–CO bonds [10]. FT-IR spectroscopy of all samples (Li-C dot, Na-C dot, and Na-C dot) revealed the presence of CC structure and various oxygen functional groups within the C dot. Specifically, a broad stretching band for hydroxyl and carboxylic groups was observed around 3416 cm−1, CO stretching of carboxyl groups around 1783 cm−1, aromatic CC stretching around 1637 cm−1, OC stretching of carboxyl groups and CH bending around 1355 cm−1, and CO stretching of epoxy groups on the basal plane around 1300 and 1080 cm−1, as shown in Fig. 1b and Fig. S1 [13,14]. XPS was performed to determine the composition of the prepared samples, as shown in Fig. 1c and Fig. S2. All prepared samples exhibited peaks around 285.5 eV and 288.3 eV, corresponding to CO and O–CO groups, respectively, located at the edges of the C dot [14,21]. Additionally, K 2p, Na 1 s, and Li 1 s spectra were observed in K-C dot, Na-C dot, and Li-C dot, respectively. On the other hand, no XPS spectra of metal cations were detected in samples composed only of C dot, as shown in Fig. S2 c and S2d. These results indicate that various types of oxygen-containing functional groups, such as CO and O–CO, are decorated on the edge of the C dot. Consequently, this suggests that ionic complexes were formed through electrostatic interactions and/or π-cation interactions between the metal cations and the C dot [16,17,21]. To investigate the electrolyte properties of ionic complexes based on C dot, an electrochemical deposition method was used to fabricate a PB thin film on an ITO/glass substrate [2]. The UV–Vis spectrum of PB/ITO exhibits a strong absorption peak at 700 nm, as shown in Fig. 2a, which is associated with metal-to-ligand-to-metal charge transfer from Fe2+ into low-spin sites to Fe3+ into high-spin sites within the PB structure [5,18,19]. The absorbance at 700 nm increased linearly with the extending electrodeposition time of PB, as depicted in Fig. 2b. These results indicate that a uniformly grown PB thin film was electrodeposited. The morphology of PB thin films, which influences mass transport dynamics and heterogeneous electron transfer rates, was assessed using SEM. PB/ITO shows an aggregated morphology with several hundred nanometers in size, as shown in Fig. 3 . The electrochemical behavior of PB/ITO (electrodeposition condition: 40 µA, 140 s) was investigated for different electrolyte types, including Li-C dot, Na-C dot, and K-C dot, using commercially available KCl as a control. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed using a three-electrode system, consisting of working electrode (PB thin film), counter electrode (Pt wire), and reference electrode (Ag/AgCl). The color change of the PB thin films occurs upon the intercalation/deintercalation of counter cations into the lattice channels of PB structure during redox reactions of PB. Diffusion of counter cations into the PB lattice structure can be expressed as [5,20];
Fe
4
III

C N

C l

-

The electrochemical behavior of PB thin films shows two redox pair reactions regardless of electrolyte species. Interestingly, different redox behaviors were observed in PB/ITO depending on the type of electrolyte used. The resulting CV curve obtained from PB/ITO using K-C dots shows two well-defined reversible redox couples, as shown in Fig. 4 a. On the other hand, when Li-C dot, Na-C dot, and KCl were used, cyclic voltammograms exhibited relatively slow and irreversible redox reactions, resulting in a large peak-to-peak separation (ΔEpp), as depicted in Fig. S3. This is because the diffusion process of alkali cations such as K+, Na+, and Li+ dominates the redox reactions of PB, and the charge transfer between PB and alkali cations is constrained by the channel radius of the PB structure. To further study the redox reaction kinetics of PB thin films, EIS was performed. The Nyquist plot of the PB thin films displayed similar series resistance values associated with the electrolyte's electrical resistance. However, the semicircle related to charge transfer resistance (Rct) observed in the high-frequency region differed in shape, as depicted in Fig. 4b and summarized in Table S1. The order of Rct decreased in the order of Li-C dot (359 Ω) > Na-C dot (285 Ω) > KCl (62 Ω) > K-C dot (52 Ω). This is determined by ion mobility, which follows the reverse order of ionic radius and increases with the hydration radius (Li+, 3.82 Å; Na+, 3.58 Å; K+, 3.31 Å) [22,23]. Furthermore, the hydrated Li+ and Na+ cations are too large to migrate easily into and out of the PB lattice and might also strongly interact with the reduced Fe2+ state within the Prussian Blue lattice. On the other hand, K+ cations with small hydrated ionic radius migrate more efficiently through the lattice channels of the PB structure [24–26]. Furthermore, the slope of the straight line in the lower frequency region indicates the mass transfer of cations toward the PB thin film from the electrolyte solution. The diffusion resistance of the K+ cations was relatively low compared to that of the Na+ and Li+ cations. The rate-determining step in the charge-transfer kinetics of the electrochromic process for PB thin films depends on the migration of counter cations insertion and extraction of the PB lattice channels to maintain charge balance. As a result, the electrochemical studies involving CV and EIS have shown that the K-C dot serves as a suitable material for an electrolyte. The electrochromic characteristics of PB thin films, including optical density (OD), coloration efficiency (η), and switching cycle stability, were evaluated using the CA method under alternating voltage biases of –0.16 V and 0.4 V for 10 s each in a three-electrode system. Electrochromic behaviors of PB thin films exhibited a bleaching state at a bias of –0.16 V. Thus, K+ cations migrated into the lattice channel of reduced PB (indicates PW) to maintain the electroneutrality of the PB thin film. After changing the applied voltage to 0.4 V, the PW returned to the coloration state (indicates PB). Therefore, through the oxidation process involving the move out of K+ cations from the lattice channels of the PW structure, the PW structure reverts to its initial state to PB structure. The key parameter η in the electrochromic process is determined by the charge transfer between the PB thin film and the electrode, as well as the migration of cations intercalated and deintercalated with PB structure [10]. η is calculated as Equation (1) [10].
(1)
η
( λ )
=
Δ O D
Δ Q
=
l o g (
T b
T c
)
Δ Q
where optical density (OD) is the logarithm of the ratio of transmittance between the bleached and colored states. T c and T b represent the transmittance values corresponding to 10 % and 90 % of the total modulation at 700 nm absorbance, respectively. The measured parameter values related to the electrochromic characteristics of each electrolyte are summarized in Table 1 . When using K-C dot, all parameter values were better than those of other electrolytes. The η and ΔOD values were measured to be 103.5 cm2 C−1 and 0.90, respectively. The color switching time was measured using the CA method under alternating voltage biases of –0.16 and 0.4 V for 10 s. The t c (time for coloring process) and t b (time for bleaching process) correspond to the switching times when the transmittance modulation of the PB thin film at 700 nm reaches 10 % and 90 %, respectively, as shown in Fig. S4. Under K-C dot, the PB thin films exhibited faster color switching transitions (t b = 1.5 s, t c = 2.6 s) compared to Li-C dot, Na-C dot, and KCl. Moreover, the switching cycle stability related to the number of color switching exhibited only a slight degradation of approximately 3 % (ΔT, from 76 to 74) after 1,000 switching cycles, as depicted in Fig. 5 a. These results indicate that the electrochromic properties of the PB thin film exhibit high durability by maintaining minimal performance loss throughout the switching cycles. However, Li-C dot and Na-C dot exhibited lower electrochromic properties of PB thin films compared to K-C dot. This is because Li+ and Na+ cations with large hydrated ionic radius have difficulty intercalation and deintercalation into the PB lattice channel during redox reactions of PB. Consequently, PB thin films using Li-C dot and Na-C dot struggled to maintain electrical neutrality, resulting in side reactions and a subsequent degradation of electrochromic performance [24,25]. These results suggest that enhancing electrochromic performance in ECDs requires balancing two critical factors: the migration of counter cations to maintain the charge neutrality of PB and the electron exchange between PB and the electrode. Note that PB thin films using KCl showed significant degradation in electrochromic performance after 130 coloration switching cycles, as shown in Fig. 5b. It is known that PB is chemically attacked by Cl− anions, leading to degradation and undesirable side reactions [6]. In contrast, nanosized C dot are too large to enter the PB lattice structure and remain relatively stable in their anionic state, thus not inducing side reactions of PB [10]. As a result, the K-C dot facilitated the redox reactions in PB thin films, leading to high coloration efficiency, good optical density, fast color switching time, and long-term switching stability.

In summary, we demonstrate ionic complexes based on C dot as an electrolyte. The C dot formed an ionic complex with alkali cations, such as Li+, Na+, and K+, through electrostatic attraction and/or π–cation interaction. These prepared ionic complexes based on C dot as an electrolyte provide abundant free metal cations, highly stable polyanions, and negligible ion pairs in an aqueous solution. However, when used as electrolytes in the electrochemical reactions of PB thin films, Li+ and Na+ cations in Li-C dot and Na-C dot, respectively, cause slow and irreversible redox reactions in CV, as well as high Rct values in EIS, leading to the degradation of the electrochromic performance of PB thin films. Note that the K-GQD electrolyte demonstrates superior electrochromic performance, including excellent coloration efficiency (η = 103.5 cm2 C−1), high optical contrast (ΔOD = 0.90), and fast color switching time (t b = 1.5 and t c = 2.6 s). compared to conventional KCl electrolyte. Moreover, K-C dot exhibits excellent long-term switching cycle stability, with an optical transmittance retention of 97.3 % (ΔT1 cycle = 76 % and ΔT1000 cycles = 74 %) over 1000 switching cycles. All the presented results suggest that improving electrochromic performance in ECDs requires careful balancing of two critical aspects: ensuring charge neutrality through the migration of counter ions within the electrochromic material, and facilitating effective electron transfer between the electrochromic material and the electrode. We anticipate that ionic complexes based on C dot will prove to be promising electrolytes for various electrochemical cells, including secondary ion batteries, supercapacitors, and electrochemical transistors.
The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

Jaeheon Kim: Data curation, Investigation. Woojun Choi: Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Tae Hyun Kim: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision. Hong Chul Lim: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF), with the grant funded by Korea Government (MSIT and MOE) (No. NRF-2022R1F1A1064007). This research was also supported by “Regional Innovation Strategy (RIS)” through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (MOE) in 2024(2022RIS-005). This work was also supported by the Soonchunhyang University Research Fund.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: FT-IR data of Li-C dot and Na-C dot; XPS data of Li-C dot, Na-C dot, and C-dot; comparison of CV curves for electrodeposited PB/ITO (Li-C dot, Na-C dot, and KCl); comparison of diffusion coefficient of alkali cations and EIS data; comparison of electrochromic properties of PB/ITO. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2024.118676.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A red, green, and blue (RGB) polymeric electrochromic device (PECD): The dawning of the PECD era

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Sonmez, C. K. F. Shen, Y. Rubin, F. Wudl, A red, green, and blue (RGB) polymeric electrochromic device (PECD): The dawning of the PECD era, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 43 (2004) 1498–1502.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrodeposition of zinc oxide nanowires as a counter electrode in electrochromic devices

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. C. Lim, E. Park, I.-S. Shin, Electrodeposition of zinc oxide nanowires as a counter electrode in electrochromic devices, Bull. Korean Chem. Soc. 41 (2020) 358–361.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Integrated energy storage and electrochromic function in one flexible device: an energy storage smart window

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Wang, H. Wu, Y. Meng. Y. Zhang and Z. Wei, Integrated energy storage and electrochromic function in one flexible device: an energy storage smart window, Energy Environ. Sci. 5 (2012) 8384–8389.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Transparent Zinc-Mesh Electrodes for Solar-Charging Electrochromic Windows

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Li, W. Zhang, A. Y. Elezzabi, Transparent Zinc-Mesh Electrodes for Solar-Charging Electrochromic Windows, Adv. Mater. (2020) 2003574.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhancing electrocatalytic performance and stability: A novel Prussian blue-graphene quantum dot nanoarchitecture for H2O2 reduction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. C. Lim, Y. Cho, D. Han, T. H. Kim, Enhancing electrocatalytic performance and stability: A novel Prussian blue-graphene quantum dot nanoarchitecture for H2O2 reduction, Appl. Surf. Sci. 646 (2024) 158920.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Transition metal hexacyanoferrates in electrocatalysis of H2O2 reduction: An exclusive property of Prussian blue

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. A. Sitnikova, M. A. Komkova, I. V. Khomyakova, E. E. Karyakina, A. A. Karyakin, Transition metal hexacyanoferrates in electrocatalysis of H2O2 reduction: An exclusive property of Prussian blue, Anal. Chem. 86 (2014) 4131−4134.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Stability of PB films on ITO electrodes: effect of different anions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Roig, J. Navarro, R. Tamarit, F. Vicente, Stability of PB films on ITO electrodes: effect of different anions, J. Electroanal. Chem. 360 (1993) 55−69.
R. D. Rogers, K. R. Sedden, ionic liquids--solvents of the future?, Science 302 (2003) 792–793.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Progress and perspectives on halide lithium conductors for all-solid-state lithium batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Li, J. Liang, X. Yang, K. R. Adair, C. Wang, F. Zhao, X. Sun, Progress and perspectives on halide lithium conductors for all-solid-state lithium batteries, Energy Environ. Sci. 13 (2020) 1429–1461.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: New highly stable ionic compounds composed of multivalent graphene quantum dot anions and alkali metal cations

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. C. Lim, M.-C. Kim, A. Kim, E. Park, Y. Park, R. Chang, J.-I. Hong, K.-W. Park, I.-S. Shin, H. Kim, New highly stable ionic compounds composed of multivalent graphene quantum dot anions and alkali metal cations, Batter. Supercaps 5 (2021) e202100337.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal complexes of weakly coordinating anions. precursors of strong cationic organometallic lewis acids

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Beck, K. Sunkel, Metal complexes of weakly coordinating anions. precursors of strong cationic organometallic lewis acids, Chem. Rev. 88 (2010) 2146–2149.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The search for larger and more weakly coordinating anions

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review of electrolyte materials and composition for electrochemical supercapacitors
